title: Mega Mailbag: ‘She Said,’ Best Journalism Movies, and an Oscars Power Ranking
format: Sean and Amanda bring in PressBox's Brian Curtis to review 'She Said,' weigh its Oscar odds against the rest of the fall slate, and open the mailbag on the best and worst journalism movies.

films.title: She Said
films.note: Maria Schrader directs Rebecca Lenkiewicz's adaptation of the Kantor-Twohey book; Carey Mulligan as Megan Twohey, Zoe Kazan as Jodi Kantor. Shot inside the actual New York Times building. Praised for the Samantha Morton and Jennifer Ehle source scenes; dinged for over-explaining and keeping Harvey Weinstein off screen (a Gwyneth Paltrow soundalike turns up on one phone call).

segments.heading: Where 'She Said' sits in the Oscar race
segments.note: Plan B, category fraud, and the adapted-screenplay field

films.title: All the President's Men
films.note: The constant touchstone: the greatest journalism movie, per Brian, and the model 'She Said' both borrows from and lives in the shadow of.

films.title: Network
films.note: Cited for Beatrice Straight, who won Best Supporting Actress on roughly two scenes: the precedent for a Samantha Morton or Jennifer Ehle push.

films.title: Black Panther: Wakanda Forever
films.note: Angela Bassett floated as a strong Supporting Actress contender likely to crowd out 'She Said'.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: Jamie Lee Curtis named among the crowded Supporting Actress field.

films.title: Women Talking
films.note: Sarah Polley's adaptation of the Miriam Toews novel; multiple eligible performances and an adapted-screenplay rival.

films.title: The Whale
films.note: A play adaptation, so it counts in the adapted-screenplay conversation.

films.title: Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery
films.note: Netflix's $400M sequel; premiered at the Academy Museum and looms over the adapted-screenplay category.

films.title: Knives Out
films.note: The original. Brian's the dissenter, calling it too mannered and weird.

films.title: Living
films.note: The Sundance film adapting Kurosawa; Kazuo Ishiguro's script is an adapted-screenplay entry.

films.title: Ikiru
films.note: The Kurosawa source 'Living' remakes.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Eligible for adapted screenplay as a legacy sequel; a Top Gun win would 'tell you everything' about the category.

films.title: Top Gun
films.note: The characters 'Maverick' is adapted from, hence the adapted-screenplay eligibility.

films.title: Page One: Inside the New York Times
films.note: The doc that makes the real Times newsroom recognizable, a useful reference for how 'She Said' films the building.

segments.heading: The journalism-movie mailbag
segments.note: best, worst, and the writing that never happens on screen

films.title: Spotlight
films.note: Held up as the model editor movie: Michael Keaton looking at you and distracted at once.

films.title: Almost Famous
films.note: William Miller getting fact-checked; a rare on-screen nod to the process.

films.title: Shattered Glass
films.note: Brian's pick for the best film on magazine process, thanks to the opening monologue on how a story moves from writer to editor.

films.title: Absence of Malice
films.note: Brian's nominee for worst journalism: Sally Field's reporter takes wildly unethical shortcuts against Paul Newman.

films.title: Never Been Kissed
films.note: Drew Barrymore goes undercover at her old high school; the ethics do not survive scrutiny.

films.title: Die Hard
films.note: Sean's bad-journalism pick: William Atherton's reporter exposes McClane's wife and endangers her.

films.title: Die Hard 2
films.note: The payoff: Holly McClane decks the same reporter on the plane. 'That's called arc.'

films.title: Citizen Kane
films.note: Brian's counter to the 'old movies sanitized journalism' premise: it's about manipulating the press.

films.title: The Front Page
films.note: The screwball newspaper comedy Brian says the genre lost, alongside 'His Girl Friday'.

films.title: His Girl Friday
films.note: The other screwball-newsroom touchstone for a genre that migrated to TV and ad-agency stories.

films.title: Sleepless in Seattle
films.note: Meg Ryan's character is a journalist, one of the '90s rom-com reporters.

films.title: How to Lose a Guy in 10 Days
films.note: Kate Hudson at a women's magazine, running the Matthew McConaughey stunt: the reporter-sleeps-with-the-subject trope.

films.title: Foreign Correspondent
films.note: Hitchcock's; Brian loves the opening's run of jokes about how useless foreign correspondents are.

films.title: The Pelican Brief
films.note: Amanda's recurring cause: the orphaned stepchild of the Pakula paranoia trilogy, with Denzel Washington's reporter.

films.title: The Devil Wears Prada
films.note: Amanda's case for a great magazine movie: the book, the run-through, advertiser pressure, and Stanley Tucci's 'take it seriously' speech.

films.title: Natural Born Killers
films.note: Sean's media-satire pick: Robert Downey Jr.'s tabloid host as a TMZ precursor.

films.title: To Die For
films.note: The other over-the-top media satire: a murderous weather woman clawing toward serious anchoring.

films.title: Wag the Dog
films.note: Media manipulation as a tool of power.

films.title: The Paper
films.note: The tabloid-newsroom movie, again with Keaton. 900 things happen in two hours; ripe for a series remake.

films.title: Interview with the Vampire
films.note: Offered, half-joking, as real journalism: Christian Slater's reporter is why we have Louis's story.

films.title: The Harder They Fall
films.note: Humphrey Bogart as a sportswriter, top of Brian's sports-journalism list.

films.title: The Odd Couple
films.note: Oscar Madison, sportswriter.

films.title: Leatherheads
films.note: The George Clooney football movie packed with sportswriter characters.

films.title: Paper Lion
films.note: George Plimpton's participatory sportswriting, adapted.

films.title: My Best Friend's Wedding
films.note: The forgotten detail: Julia Roberts's character is a restaurant critic.

films.title: Cobb
films.note: Robert Wuhl as sportswriter Al Stump, chronicling Ty Cobb.

films.title: Hustlers
films.note: Adapted from Jessica Pressler's story; Julia Stiles plays the reporter, the framing device done well.

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
films.note: Tom Junod's real relationship with Mr. Rogers becomes the story, a framing device that earns its place.

films.title: The Secret Life of Walter Mitty
films.note: The tonal model Sean imagines for a Michael Lewis movie: episodic, a bit Peter Sellers.

films.title: Snowden
films.note: Amanda never saw it; guesses it's 'all just documents' and not more cinematic than 'She Said'.
